text.title: Solo Change Order Mini Contract — Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every change-order email must enforce. These are HARD rules — eagerness to keep the client happy cannot override them.

rule.statement: The change order MUST reference the master agreement by name + date + signed-version hash or identifier; an order that does not anchor to a parent is a new agreement, not a change.

rationale.source: Contract-law amendment doctrine (Restatement (Second) §283 + UK Misrepresentation Act)

rule.rationale: Unanchored modifications create disputes over which terms (limitation of liability, IP assignment, payment) carry through.

rule.statement: The order MUST contain four explicit deltas: scope_delta (verbatim deliverable text), price_delta (fixed sum OR hourly cap), schedule_delta (new end date or "no change"), and payment_terms_delta (net-N or up-front %); deltas that say "TBD" reject the order.

rationale.source: Construction industry AIA G701 change-order standard

rule.rationale: Ambiguous deltas are the #1 cause of post-delivery payment disputes; closed fields force the conversation while goodwill is still present.

rule.statement: The order MUST include a single-sentence acceptance clause: "Reply YES to this email to authorize this change order under the terms of the master agreement"; replies of "ok"/"sure"/"go ahead" SHOULD be accepted only when accompanied by a quote of the table.

rationale.source: E-SIGN Act (US) 15 USC §7001 + eIDAS Regulation (EU) No 910/2014

rule.rationale: A clear assent + intent-to-be-bound is the minimum threshold; ambiguous "ok" creates re-litigation risk later.

rule.statement: Work on the changed scope MUST NOT begin before the signed reply is captured and stored; if the client pressures for parallel work, the freelancer sends a one-line "starting under verbal authorization, please confirm in email today" — verbal-only work is capped at 8 hours.

rationale.source: Unauthorised-work doctrine + freelancer-collections research (Freelancers Union annual report)

rule.rationale: Verbal authorizations are unenforceable in half of jurisdictions; the 8-hour cap bounds the loss when the client ghosts.

rule.statement: The signed change order MUST be stored as an immutable artifact (PDF, email thread, signed document service entry) and referenced in the invoice line item that bills it; invoices that bill a change without referencing its identifier reject themselves.
rule.rationale: Invoices challenged at payment time win on the back of a single referenceable artifact; the link from invoice → order → master is what closes the loop.

example.title: Anchored change-order email
example.code:
Subject: Change Order CO-002 — Acme Web Portal SOW dated 2026-04-12
Hi Sam,
Per our chat today, here is the change order for the new client-export feature, applying under our master SOW dated 2026-04-12.
| Field | Delta | |-------|-------| | Scope | Add CSV export endpoint + UI button on /clients page. | | Price | +$1,200 flat (fixed). | | Schedule | New end date 2026-05-29 (+5 business days). | | Payment | Billed on completion, net-14, same terms as SOW. |
Reply YES to this email to authorize this change order under the terms of the master agreement.
— Alex
example.note: Anchored to SOW, four explicit deltas, single-sentence acceptance, no ambiguity.

example.title: Un-anchored change-order chat
example.code: "sure, can add the export thing, will throw it in this week, sort the bill at the end"
example.note: No master reference, no price delta, no schedule delta, no acceptance. The freelancer will eat this scope.
